svg.text: BRITISH SIGN LANGUAGE
CHEROLOGY — EUROPEAN SIGN LANGUAGE PARAMETERS
BSL HANDSHAPES
BSL uses many of the same handshape categories as other sign languages
Some handshapes unique to BSL: reflect BSL lexical history
Two-handed signs: dominant + non-dominant hand interact differently than ASL
BSL LOCATION AND MOVEMENT
BSL signs often more body-anchored than ASL signs
Many BSL signs: contact with specific body part (head, chest, forearm)
Movement: same categories (linear, arc, circular, wiggle) as all sign languages
BSL NON-MANUAL MARKERS
Eyebrows: same grammatical function as ASL (question marking)
Mouth patterns: BSL uses English lip patterns more than ASL uses English
English mouthing: visible with signs in BSL; varies by signer and context
BSL REGIONAL VARIATION
BSL: significant dialect variation across UK regions
London, Manchester, Edinburgh, Cardiff: different signs for common words
Color signs especially variable: no standard across UK
DGS CHEROLOGY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
DGS PARAMETERS
DGS uses same five parameters as all sign languages
DGS handshapes: some overlap with ASL (shared French SL ancestors)
DGS has compound signs: two signs combined = new meaning (common in DGS)
DGS REGIONAL VARIATION
DGS: strong dialect variation (North vs South Germany)
Hamburg variety: often taken as standard (Hamburg SL research center)
Bayern (Bavaria): notably different from Hamburg variety
EUROPEAN SIGN LANGUAGE FEATURES
BSL and DGS: both use signing space grammatically (universal)
Both: NMM mark questions, topics, negation
Both: verb agreement through spatial modification
Despite European geographic proximity: BSL and DGS are NOT mutually intelligible
PLATE 63b ALT · BSL / DGS · CHEROLOGY
